coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots and its application for detection of hydrogen peroxide by photoluminescence spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Quyen, Tran Thi Bich
My, Ngo Nguyen Tra
Pham, Duy Toan
Thien, Doan Van Hong
coredata.dc:description: TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots (TiO2 NSs/GQDs) have been successfully developed using a simple and rapid process at 60 °C for 30 min. The GQDs were obtained using a natural source of rice starch and ascorbic acid, a reducing agent, under a rapid and simple microwave-assisted synthetic method for 10 min; and TiO2 NSs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs were characterized by Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-vis), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X – ray Diffractometry (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX). The novel TiO2 NSs/GQDs were successfully synthesized, possessed an average particle size of ∼10–15 nm (TiO2 NSs) and ∼8–10 nm (GQDs), and were homogenously distributed in the dispersion. Regarding the catalytic application, the TiO2 NSs/GQDs demonstrated high catalytic and photocatalytic activity for the detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by photoluminescence spectroscopy at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, with a low H2O2 limit of detection (LOD) of 10−14 M and a wide detection range from 10−14 M to 10−2 M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots
Titanium dioxide nanosheets
Photocatalytic activity
Hydrogen peroxide
Rice starch
Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots
Preparation of TiO2 NSs/GQDs catalyst for hydrogen peroxide detection by photoluminescence spectroscopy
Preparation of hydrogen peroxide detection in actual sample by photoluminescence spectroscopy
Characterization of synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV-vis spectras (left) and the absorbance scales (right) of TiO2 nanosheets (TiO2 NSs) combined with GQDs at various amounts of TiO2 NSs: (a) 1 mL, (b) 1.5 mL, (c) 2 mL, (d) 2.5 mL, and (e) 3 mL.
(B) UV-vis spectras (left) and the absorbance scales (right) of (a) TiO2 NSs and (b) TiO2 NSs combined with GQDs.
XRD patterns of (a) TiO2 NSs/GQDs nanocomposites and (b) GQDs.
FTIR spectra of (a) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and (b) TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots (TiO2 NSs/GQDs) composites.
TEM images of (a) graphene quantum dots (GQDs); (b) TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots (TiO2 NSs/GQDs) composites and (c) particle size distribution of TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites.
EDX spectrum of TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots (TiO2 NSs/GQDs) composites.
(A) Photoluminescence spectra of (a) pure GQDs and (b) TiO2 NSs/GQDs under excitation at 340 nm.
(B) Photoluminescence spectra of (a) TiO2 NPs/GQDs and (b) TiO2 NSs/GQDs under excitation at 340 nm.
(A) The characteristic color of (a) TiO2 NSs/GQDs and (b) TiO2 NSs/GQDs with the additions of various concentrations of H2O2.
(B) Photoluminescence spectra of TiO2 NSs/GQDs with various hydrogen peroxide concentrations at the excitation at 340 nm.
(C) Photoluminescence spectra (left) of (a) blood sample and (b) TiO2 NSs/GQDs and the calibration curve of the developed TiO2 NSs/GQDs towards different samples of H2O2 (right)

head.ce:title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots and its application for detection of hydrogen peroxide by photoluminescence spectroscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, College of Technology, Can Tho University, 3/2 Street, Ninh Kieu District, Can Tho, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemical Engineering, College of Technology, Can Tho University, 3/2 Street, Ninh Kieu District, Can Tho, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Natural Sciences, Can Tho University, 3/2 Street, Ninh Kieu District, Can Tho, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, College of Natural Sciences, Can Tho University, 3/2 Street, Ninh Kieu District, Can Tho, Viet Nam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots (TiO2 NSs/GQDs) have been successfully developed using a simple and rapid process at 60 °C for 30 min. The GQDs were obtained using a natural source of rice starch and ascorbic acid, a reducing agent, under a rapid and simple microwave-assisted synthetic method for 10 min; and TiO2 NSs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs were characterized by Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-vis), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X – ray Diffractometry (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX). The novel TiO2 NSs/GQDs were successfully synthesized, possessed an average particle size of ∼10–15 nm (TiO2 NSs) and ∼8–10 nm (GQDs), and were homogenously distributed in the dispersion. Regarding the catalytic application, the TiO2 NSs/GQDs demonstrated high catalytic and photocatalytic activity for the detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by photoluminescence spectroscopy at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, with a low H2O2 limit of detection (LOD) of 10−14 M and a wide detection range from 10−14 M to 10−2 M.

A novel, low-cost and eco-friendly method to fabricate TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots (TiO2 NSs/GQDs) composites in a simple step was proposed. Herein, GQDs were prepared using natural source from rice starch under a supporting of microwave irradiation with rapidly approach in 10 min; and TiO2 nanosheets (TiO2 NSs) synthesized by hydrothermal method were successfully combined to generate highly catalytic ability and photocatalytic activity TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites. Furthermore, the ability of TiO2 NSs/GQDs has been successfully used to detect hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The results of the luminescence spectroscopy (PL) shown the potential of TiO2 NSs/GQDs in detecting H2O2 as a sensitive sensor when reaching extremely low limit of detection (LOD) of H2O2 in solution (10−14 M) in the wide detection range from 10−14 to 10−2 M.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the research on finding H2O2 sensing nanomaterials with high sensitivity, wide-range detection ability, and environmental friendliness has receiving considerable attention from the scientific research community. Indeed, the detection of H2O2 can be applied in many fields such as food [1], biology [2], environment, and clinical diagnosis for serious diseases such as cancer, diabetes, and related neurological diseases [2–5]. Various approaches have been proposed for H2O2 detection such as luminescence spectroscopy [3,6,7], electrochemical methods [8], UV–Vis spectroscopy [9], and chromatography [10]. In particular, the fluorescence-based method is considered a potential choice because of its advantages such as high sensitivity and versatile measurability on various parameters without affecting the host system [3]. Therefore, the research and development of nanomaterials capable of detecting H2O2 by fluorescence (PL) spectroscopy have always been the top goal that scientists aim to achieve.

Photoluminescent carbonaceous dots have attracted increasing attention in the last decade due to the advantages of high photostability, high resistance against photobleaching, and excellent biocompatibility. Carbonaceous dots can be classified as nanodiamonds, carbon nanodots, or graphene quantum dots (GQDs), according to their sp2 and sp3 hybridization composition [11]. These nanomaterials have been proved to be potential candidates for biolabeling and bioimaging in-vitro or in-vivo [12–15], nanovehicles for biomolecular delivery [16], and biosensors for probing DNA, protein and intracellular metal ions [17–20], as well as photocatalysts, electrocatalysts, or photovoltaic devices for energy conversion (graphene sheets with sizes about or below 100 nm possess isolated energy levels) [21–24]. The photoluminescence of GQDs is considered to be originated from the quantum size effect along with different emissive traps on the GQD surface [25,26]. Generally, there are two ways to synthesize GQDs, including the bottom-up route by pyrolysis of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons or cyclodehydrogenation of polyphenylene precursors [27,28], and the top-down route by chemical cutting, electrochemical tailoring, or solvo-thermal processes [29–33]. However, a facile and eco-friendly synthesis of novel GQDs is still a challenge. Therefore, further exploration of this novel nanomaterial for its widespread application is necessary.

Moreover, the combination of organic–inorganic nanoparticles composition (hybrids) have attracted much interests recently due to their versatile chemical, optical, and mechanical characteristics, as well as their potential applications [34,35]. Nanotitanium dioxide (TiO2), especially the anatase form, has a high photocatalytic efficiency under UV light [36]. Thus, titania-based materials are very attractive due to their inherent high refractive index and UV absorbing properties. For instance, titania-polymer hybrids have been prepared with conductive polymers [37–39], polyacrylonitrile electrospun fibers [40], polyacrylonitrile and carbon nanotubes [41,42], block co-polymers [43–45], polystyrene beads [46,47], polyamide [48], acrylic acid or PMMA [49,50], silicates or siloxanes [51], polyimides [52], epoxies [53], and polycations [54]. With the strong movement towards biodegradable, renewable, sustainable, and carbon-neutral polymeric materials, it is also of importance to develop viable and facile production routes for nanocomposites using such biopolymers.

As known, the combination of GQDs with TiO2 nanosheets (TiO2 NSs) using a simple, rapid, low-cost, and environment-friendly synthetic method at room temperature have not been reported previously. Herein, we report such method to prepare GQDs from natural source of rice starch and ascorbic acid under the supporting of microwave irradiation. In addition, TiO2 NSs were modified on the surface by GQDs using a simple method with rapid reaction time of 30 min at 60 °C. The synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs were thoroughly characterized by Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-vis), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X – ray Diffractometry (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX). Furthermore, TiO2 NSs/GQDs were also investigated as an enzymatic replacing for peroxidase enzyme in the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the simulated body fluid (SBF) solution at pH 7.0.

Hydrofluoric acid (HF; 48%); Titanium Butoxide (Ti(Obu)4; 99%), Ascorbic acid (C6H8O6; 99,7%), and Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2; 30%) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Postassium chloride (KCl; 99%), Sodium chloride (NaCl; 99%), Sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4, 99%) and Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, 99%) were bought from Hemidia, India. Rice starch was purchased at Coop-mart supermarket in Can Tho City. All solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water from a MilliQ system. Besides, a blood sample collected at Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy was prepared for the H2O2 detection experiment.

TiO2 NSs were prepared by the reaction of Ti(OBu)4 and HF as following steps: 2 mL of HF was added into 1 mL of Ti(OBu)4 and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. The mixture was transferred into a 15 mL autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The calcined sample was then dissolved in 5 mL of DI water. After 1 h of sonication, the obtained product was centrifuged, washed several times to remove the precipitates, and stored in 10 mL DI water for further experiments.

To manufacture the GQDs, 0.15 g of rice starch and 6 mL of ascorbic acid (10% w/v) were completely dissolved in 19 mL of DI water for 15 min. The precursor solution was heated at a power level of 900 W under supporting of microwave irradiation. After 10 min under microwave radiation, 25 mL of DI water was added to the obtained dark-brown solid. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min to yield a homogeneous solution. Finally, pre-product was centrifuged, washed with DI water several times to remove the precipitates, and stored in 10 mL DI water for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanosheets/graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare TiO2 NSs/GQDs, 10 mL of GQDs were first heated at 60 °C and stirred for 15 min. Then, TiO2 NSs dispersions, at different amounts of 1; 1.5; 2; 2.5; and 3 mL, were added and constantly stirred for 30 min. The generated TiO2 NSs/GQDs were used as catalysts for hydrogen peroxide detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TiO2 NSs/GQDs catalyst for hydrogen peroxide detection by photoluminescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of TiO2 NSs/GQDs catalyst in detecting H2O2 was investigated at different concentrations of H2O2. For this, 200 μL H2O2 solution with different concentrations sequentially (10−2; 10−4; 10−5; 10−6; 10−8; 10−9; 10−12; and 10−14 M) was added to a mixture consisting of 1 mL TiO2 NSs/GQDs and 8.8 mL DI water. Then, the freshly prepared solution was sonicated for 10 min and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hydrogen peroxide detection in actual sample by photoluminescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare for H2O2 recognition in blood sample, PBS buffer was first prepared by following steps: 4 g NaCl, 0.1 g KCl; 0.72 g Na2HPO4 and 0.12 g KH2PO4 were dissolved in 400 mL of DI water. The solution was kept in 500 mL Volumetric Flask and adjusted the pH to about ∼7.4. Next, DI water was carefully added to the mark to make PBS buffer solution.

Dissolve the blood sample in PBS buffer (1:1 v/v) until a homogeneous solution is obtained. Then, 200 uL of the blood solution was added to the mixture of 1 mL TiO2 NSs/GQDs and 8.8 mL DI water. The as-prepared solution was sonicated for 10 min and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2 NSs/GQDs synthesis was observed by recording the absorbance spectra between 200 and 900 nm on the UV-vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific Evolution 60S UV-Vis spectrophotometer, USA). XRD was performed on a D8-Advance machine (Bruker, Germany) in the 2θ range of 10°–80°. The FTIR spectra were obtained by Perkin Elmer Frontier MIR/NIR (Perkin Elmer, USA), conducted with KBr pellet technique at room temperature in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. TEM characterization was performed on a Jem1010 device (Joel Company, Japan). Chemical properties and constituent components were analyzed by EDX (EDX H-7593, Horiba, England). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) was recorded at 350 nm using FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France).

Fig. 1 shows the UV-vis results of TiO2 NSs/GQDs at different volumes of TiO2 NSs. Obviously, the maximum absorption peak at 300 nm of TiO2 NSs/GQDs was clearly demonstrated and has the highest intensity corresponding to TiO2 NSs (2 mL)/GQDs sample. The characteristic absorption peak intensity of TiO2 NSs/GQDs gradually increased as the content/volume of TiO2 NSs increased to 2 mL, (Fig. 1A (a, b, c)); and decreased when the content/volume of TiO2 NSs was greater than 2 mL (Fig. 1A (d, e)). Therefore, the suitable amount of TiO2 NSs for the synthesis of TiO2 NSs/GQDs was 2 mL.

As previously mentioned, combining GQDs with TiO2 led to narrowing the bandgap of the synthesized materials Fig. 1.B shows that pure TiO2 NSs have a characteristic absorption peak at ∼310 nm. Meanwhile, the maximum absorption peak of the synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs has shifted to a smaller wavelength (∼300 nm) and has a lower bandgap energy (eV). This is probably due to the formation of bonds when GQDs interact with TiO2 NSs.

TiO2 NSs have a wide band gap of 3 eV and are only activated under UV light (< 388 nm) to produce an electron (e−) và hole pair (h+). Therefore, the combination of TiO2 NSs and GQDs leads to the enhancement and improvement of photoactivity compared with pure TiO2 NSs.

The X-ray patterns of GQDs and TiO2 NSs/GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The GQDs have an amorphous structure (Fig. 2a), which is evident with the diffraction peak at 2θ = ∼28°, corresponding to the (002) plane of the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in GQDs (in agreement with the standard samples of GQDs - JCPDS Card No. 75-162) [55]. The position and intensity of this diffraction peak of GQDs are highly dependent on the presence of hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxylic groups, which tend to increase the distance between the carbon sheets.

Besides, XRD analysis of TiO2 NSs/GQDs in Fig. 2b shows anatase TiO2-specific diffraction peaks at 2θ = 17°, 26°, 38°, 48°, and 59°, assigning as facets of (101), (103), (004), (112), (200) (corresponding to the standard sample of TiO2 - JCPDS No. 21-1272). This change in the XRD pattern of GQDs presents that TiO2 NSs were successfully combined with GQDs and fabricated TiO2 NSs/GQDs materials.

FTIR was used to study the chemical groups of GQDs and their transformation when adding TiO2 NSs to form TiO2 NSs/GQDs. Firstly, the GQDs sample, shown in Fig. 3 a, features broad vibrations at 3427 cm−1, which are assigned to O-H stretching vibrational absorptions. A sharp vibrational peak around 2300–3000 cm−1, corresponding to the C-H bond, representing the aliphatic -CH2 and -CH3 groups in the GQDs. The oscillation peaks at 1700 and 1242 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of C=O, COO- groups; peaks at 1077 and 867 cm−1 are related to C-OH bonds and epoxy groups, respectively.

Based on the results of Fig. 3b, the TiO2 NSs/GQDs sample, the characteristic peaks for QGDs are still present and slightly changed: the broad absorption band at 3427 cm−1 has turned to a sharp peak at 3432 cm−1 due to the compression of -OH group. This band also shows that there are numerous hydroxyl and amino groups present on the surface of TiO2 NSs/GQDs [56]. The band at 1700 cm−1 was shifted to 1659 cm−1, indicated that the C=O stretching vibration in COOH functional group has participated in the complexation process. The peak appeared at 1457 cm−1 due to the C–O bond stretching in GQDs. The interaction between GQDs and TiO2 NSs led to the formation of Ti-O-C bond in TiO2 NSs/GQDs materials. Broad absorption bands at 720 cm−1 and 552 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibration of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C. These obtained results indicate that TiO2 NSs and GQDs have strong reciprocal interaction.

The morphology of the synthesized GQDs and TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites is illustrated in Fig. 4 , TEM images. The GQDs are spherical in shape, with an average size of ∼8–10 nm. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4b, TiO2 nanoparticles are in the form of sheet with an average size of ∼10–15 nm. Conclusively, TiO2 NSs have been successfully combined with GQDs to produce uniformly dispersed and non-agglomerated TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites.

To determine the composition of the synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites, EDX was employed (Fig. 5 ). The obtained results show that TiO2 NSs/GQDs contain the main components of C (44.09%), O (49.74%), and Ti (6.17%). Obviously, the synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs contain components of Ti (presented in TiO2 NSs) and C (presented in GQDs). This is consistent with the analytical results of FTIR spectra, XRD patterns, and TEM images of the synthesized TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites under optimal conditions.

The recombination rate between photo-induced electrons and holes is an important factor for the photoactivity of TiO2/GQDs composites [57]. To this end, optical fluorescence spectroscopy (luminescence spectroscopy) (PL) can directly elucidate the behaviors of photo-induced electrons and holes and highlight their radiative recombination. In general, the PL emission signals are due to the induced optical carrier recombination process. The incorporation of GQDs into TiO2 can decrease the recombination rate. Therefore, the PL intensity signal of TiO2/GQDs composites is lower.

Utilizing this mechanism, this study first compared the spherical shaped TiO2 nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs)/GQDs and TiO2 NSs/GQDs for their luminescence intensity/signal. The TiO2/GQDs composites luminescent with higher luminescence intensity were selected as the optimal TiO2/GQDs applied in the detection of H2O2 by PL in further experiments.

As shown in Fig. 6 , TiO2/GQDs have an emission (luminescence) wavelength of approximately 430 nm under excitation at 340 nm. Result shows that the PL spectra of pure GQDs has lower luminescence intensity than those of TiO2 NSs/GQDs samples (Fig. 6A). This indicates that there is an interaction between GQDs and TiO2 NSs that creates new defect states that can prevent the recombination of the electron-hole pair of nano TiO2 [58]. From there, the optical activity of the composite is also increased. Besides, Fig. 6B shows the lower intensity in the PL spectrum of TiO2 NPs/GQDs compared with TiO2 NSs/GQDs. This is probably because of the less stable and less heat-resistant TiO2 NPs. In addition, the octahedrons are in side-by-side contact, and the high energy bandgap of 3.23 eV can also affect the properties of TiO2 NPs. Meanwhile, TiO2 NSs are considered as the most stable form of TiO2 with tighter stacking than TiO2 NPs, stable cubic structure, and high heat resistance, lower energy gap width (3.0 eV). Therefore, TiO2 NSs/GQDs were selected for H2O2 detection.

This study proposed a novel sensor using TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites to detect H2O2. The sensor works on the following principles: (1) TiO2 NSs are oxidized by H2O2 because the standard reduction potential of Ti3+/TiO2 is lower than that of H2O2/H2O
; (2) reaction (1) causes TiO2 NSs to be converted to Ti3+, which means that the plasmon spectrum of TiO2 NSs in TiO2 NSs/GQDs solution is gradually reduced correspondingly to the present H2O2 concentrations (low H2O2 concentrations change the characteristic brown color of TiO2 NSs/GQDs (Fig. 7 A (a)) to light yellow, while high H2O2 concentrations make the solution become colorless). This is considered an indicator signal. In addition, TiO2 NSs act as both a capture probe and a signal probe, and this type of sensor is called direct and label-free sensor. Therefore, TiO2 NSs/GQDs act as a sensitive probe as well as an indicator for the detection of H2O2.

Expectedly, Fig. 7B shows that the PL spectral intensity of TiO2 NSs/GQDs composites changes with different H2O2 concentrations. The PL spectra have a wideband spectral shape in the range from 380 to 650 nm. The higher the concentration of H2O2, the lower the PL signal intensity of TiO2 NSs/GQDs. In other words, when the concentration of added H2O2 was lower, the difference in optical emissivity at 430 nm in the presence and absence of H2O2 of TiO2 NSs/GQDs sample was greater.

The high optical properties of TiO2 NSs/GQDs were also investigated in the detection of H2O2 concentrations in actual sample - shown in Fig. 7C. The linearity of the TiO2 NSs/GQDs towards H2O2 was in the concentration range from 10−14 to 10−2M with linear regression equation has the correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.975) where I0 and I are the relative fluorescence emission intensity of TiO2 NSs/GQDs samples in the absence and presence of H2O2. From the Calibration curve, the calculated concentration of H2O2 in the blood sample as 2.8 μM which is within the normal range of plasma H2O2 concentrations (1–5 μM) [59]. In conclusion, synthetized TiO2 NSs/GQDs can be used as H2O2 sensing materials with potential applications in clinical diagnosis with high sensitivity and wide detection range.

In this study, novel TiO2 NSs/GQDs composite was successfully fabricated with simple and eco-friendly method, and fully characterized. The material showed much potential when being applied as a sensor for the detection of H2O2 over a wide detection range of 10−2–10−14M. Indeed, TiO2 NSs/GQDs can be used as a sensitive probe to monitors the presence of H2O2 in solution. Conclusively, the TiO2 NSs/GQDs composite is a promising novel nanomaterial for applications in many fields such as chemical/electrochemical sensors, biosensors, and photocatalysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by Vietnam Ministry of Education and Training under grant number B2022-TCT-08.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2022.100103.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pt-MWCNT modified carbon electrode strip for rapid and quantitative detection of H2O2 in food

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.C. Chou, K.Y. Wu, F.X. Hsu, C.K. Lee, Pt-MWCNT modified carbon electrode strip for rapid and quantitative detection of H2O2 in food, J. Food Drug Anal.26(2) (2018) 662–669. 10.1016/j.jfda.2017.08.005

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile one-pot synthesis of copper sulfide-decorated reduced graphene oxide composites for enhanced detecting of H2O2 in biological environments

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of hexahedral Au-Pd/graphene nanocomposites biosensor and its application in cancer cell H2O2 detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monitoring the fluctuation of hydrogen peroxide in diabetes and its complications with a novel near-infrared fluorescent probe

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emission reverse change ratio photoluminescence sensor based on a probe of nitrogen-doped Ti3C2 quantum dots@DAP to detect H2O2 and xanthine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Lu, J. Wang, B. Li, C. Weng, X. Li, W. Yang, X. Yan, J. Hong, W. Zhu, X. Chem.92(11) (2020) 7770–7777. 10.1021/acs.analchem.0c00895

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanorods modified Eu: Y2O3 dispersed PVA film as a highly sensitive plasmon-enhanced luminescence probe for excellent and fast non-enzymatic detection of H2O2 and glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Kumar, A. Dwivedi, M. Srivastava, A. Srivastava, A. Srivastava, S.
